titre: Why Korea is so Good at League of Legends
transcription: Korea has won Worlds 10 times in 15 seasons. This year at Worlds, three out of the four teams in the semi-finals were all Korean. Players from around the world revere the Korean server as peak League of Legends, and teams around the world import Korean players for their high level of skill. But why? Why is Korea so good at League of Legends? Is there something in the water? Are they simply built different, or is their solo queue just that much better than every other region? Well, it actually goes much deeper than just their gameplay. Let me show you why the country of South Korea became the perfect storm to create the greatest players in League of Legends history. It's July 1997, and foreign investors have gotten cold feet with their investments in Thailand. The Thai government was forced to let the value of the Thai baht be decided by the market instead of being tied to the US dollar. And so, the Thai baht begins to collapse. This creates a ripple effect for the whole Asia region, as foreign debt-to-GDP ratios rose from 100% to 167%. The Indonesian rupiah falls alongside it. Then, the third country to feel the brunt of the Asian financial crisis was none other than South Korea. Where the South Korean won plummeted in value. Businesses in South Korea were operating on cheap credit and foreign investment, and they weren't getting their expected returns. This financial disaster left South Korea in a precarious position, unable to repay their debts to foreign investors. Luckily, the International Monetary Fund stepped in to save South Korea from this crisis with a $58 billion bailout package. But this bailout package wasn't without conditions. The IMF wanted to restructure and place conditions on South Korea to ensure that this bailout package would be repaid in the future. And although this was a dark time for Korea's financial state, the country was able to recover very quickly. Korea became a blank canvas with new regulations, ready for investment and a new identity. Korea looked forwards and understood that they couldn't compete with China on manufacturing. Instead, investing in technology. This caused Korea to implement the Cyber Korea 21 Plan. This plan states that all Korean people, regardless of age, sex, region, and income, should have the opportunity to use computers and the internet. Korea began construction of a fiber optic backbone network for the country, as well as providing high-speed networks in rural areas. This allowed Korea to bridge the geographical digital divide between urban and rural areas, ensuring that the entire country has access to high-speed internet. In order to promote PC ownership, an internet PC project was created, offering second-hand PCs free of charge to people with low incomes. There were also many Korean internet companies competing for market share, like Korea's Thrunet, Hanaro Telecom, and very, very familiar names of SK Telecom and KT. Otherwise known as Korean Telecom. This led to internet usage in Korea to absolutely skyrocket. And by 2004, 73% of Koreans were using the internet. But this wasn't just average internet. The fiber optic network made Korea one of the leading nations in high-speed internet by 2004. In just four short years, Korea had digitized both their population and their economy. After the financial crisis, many Koreans tried to start new businesses that were affordable enough for the struggling Korean populace, but also profitable and with widespread appeal. In this digitized Korea with an abundance of electronics and policies promoting PC ownership, Koreans could bulk buy PCs and peripherals, then charge customers to use them on high-speed internet. And thus, the PC bang was born. Also known as internet cafes in other countries, PC bangs started popping up all over the country, peaking at around 21,549 PC bangs in 2009. During this PC bang peak, the community and culture of PC bangs was intertwined with teenage life. PC bang owners made sure it was extremely affordable to play, charging as little as 1,000 to 1,500 won, or around 75 cents to 1 US dollar per hour. This meant that teenagers with very little money and unemployed people struggling during the financial crisis could easily afford time at the PC bang. Another reason that PC bangs thrived was because of the density of Korea. South Korea's population density is approximately 530 people per square kilometer, or around 1,375 people per square mile, making it one of the most densely populated countries in the world. I saw an Inven Global post talking about the density of Korea and how it impacted them while growing up. They're quoted in saying, "When playing at school, you'll usually see more than 100 students playing at two goal posts. More than five balls are kicked from one place to the other. Essentially, more than five games are played in the same football field at the same time." This population density impacts the ability for people to use open social spaces. But with a PC bang, each person takes up very little space. This means that multiple groups of friends can all enjoy spending time together easily, rather than taking up multiple football fields, making PC bangs a much easier place to hang out. Many Koreans have spent a significant amount of their youth hanging out at the PC bang with friends after school or university. If you think about gaming in a country without popular internet cafes, many people will own and enjoy different games. Some people grew up playing story games, some playing fighting games, or some playing first-person shooters. It just depended on what you owned. But in Korea, the PC bang culture meant that the games that were already installed on PCs would become the most popular in Korea. All of them online live service games, like Lineage, MapleStory, and most iconic of all, StarCraft: Brood War. StarCraft being an online competitive game took advantage of this Korean landscape, as the country was covered in high-speed internet, and there were lots of people in the PC bangs all playing StarCraft. The extremely rewarding mastery curve and high skill ceiling of StarCraft meant that the unemployed people from the financial crisis and the youth could invest so much time into the game and see it pay off. The high-speed internet of Korea also meant that the optimization and micro-level mechanics could be performed extremely precisely, allowing Korean players a great environment to master the game. Easy matchmaking with the Battle.net client and even an option to play LAN, allowing for local competition in your PC bang community. So, although the game wasn't even language localized on release, StarCraft took over Korean culture. By July of 2001, South Korea had purchased 2 million units of StarCraft, accounting for 50% of all StarCraft sales worldwide. StarCraft had a built-in leaderboard, and at the local PC bangs, players would crowd around the best players' screens to watch them compete against other top-level players. Noticing this, PC bang owners would put on local tournaments to decide who the best players in each district were. These small local tournaments would be the spark that eventually grew into the fire of the massive Korean eSports scene. Blizzard would then go on to host online ladder tournaments amongst the top players in each region. eSports leagues, like the Korean Professional Gamer League and Netclub, would be created, showcasing the best StarCraft players going head-to-head against one another. And South Korea as a country would fully embrace this. In the year 2000, Korea saw the future in eSports, and would establish KeSPA, otherwise known as the Korean eSports Association. The official goal when creating KeSPA was to make eSports an official sporting event and to solidify the commercial position of eSports in all sectors. This ensured legitimacy for the working conditions of pro gamers, the formation of official eSports events, and encouraging the general Korean population to play games. This led into the first official eSports TV channel called OnGameNet, which would host StarLeague, being the first professional StarCraft league in Korea, which went on to become extremely popular. In October of 2000, the world first Cyber Games was held in Korea with 174 competitors from 17 different countries, featuring games like Quake 3, Age of Empires 2, and most importantly of all, StarCraft: Brood War. The actions of KeSPA and the professional StarCraft scene in Korea started the legitimization of professional gaming all the way back in the year 2000. And by 2002, Korea had officially recognized professional gamer as a legitimate job title. These early Korean StarCraft tournaments looked so similar to the current tournaments we have today, with players on stage, big crowds, and even professional business sponsors. The only difference was back then, some of the players had way crazier entrances and celebrations. Meanwhile, esports tournaments in the west looked more like this. Foldout tables lined with hundreds of PCs, far less professional, no stages, no sponsors, and no theatrics. Don't get me wrong, I love this organic competition as well, just a bunch of gamers coming together to see who's the best. But it's clear to see that Korea was already years ahead with how esports was run and respected, all because of the development of KeSPA. The same goes for teams and players. Organizations like SKT T1 and KT Rolster got their start in the StarCraft scene. As the scene progressed, teams began to get full gaming houses, coaching staffs, managers, and corporate sponsors. This seriousness allowed Korean teams to get insanely good at StarCraft. Living with one another, discussing strategy, analyzing opponents, and having many different perspectives on the game, all coming together to create immaculate strategy. The collective intelligence of the team was far greater than the single intelligence of one player, making these teams improve exponentially. Players had scheduled practice, scrim partners, internal leagues, and would play the game 10 to 14 hours a day. The team houses and PC bangs of Korea meant that the players were not just having competition to be better than their opponent, but also trying to be better than the other players on their own team or their local area. Players like Boxer, Anytime, and Flash would not only be respected by their peers, but also the wider player base, with huge crowds of fans cheering them on as they played against one another. It wasn't instantaneous, but Korea as a country slowly gained respect for the skill it takes to be a professional gamer. All this effort and grinding paid off for the Korean players. This is the 50 highest earning StarCraft: Brood War players of all time. If you look closely, you may notice that there are quite a few Korean players on the list, with only two non-Korean players in the top 50. And all of this esports success occurred before League of Legends was released. It actually took quite a while for League of Legends to come to Korea. League of Legends was originally released on the 27th of October, 2009, with the American servers. Then the European servers were created halfway through 2010. So by the time Season 1 Worlds happened on the 18th of June, 2011, Korea still didn't have their own servers and didn't have a team to compete. But finally, after Season 1 Worlds, in December of 2011, Riot would strike a deal to bring League of Legends to South Korea. But entering the Korean market is no easy feat, as PC bangs already have games installed on their computers. So instead of trying to entice new customers with a shiny new product, you are competing against a whole roster of existing, established games. The other issue that League of Legends faced when entering the Korean market is they had almost no competitive edge. Because of Korea's high-speed internet and PC bang culture, they almost exclusively play online free games. But Riot Games approached Korea in a very respectful and sincere way, doing press conferences, talking about providing high levels of service with League of Legends when bringing it to Korea, making sure that they were providing a great player experience rather than just another online free-to-play game like all the other games in Korea. Seeing this, the Korean player base felt the sincerity of Riot Games. And after just 6 days of League of Legends being released, it had already broken into the top 10 on the list of games in Korea. The Korean player base couldn't get enough of League of Legends. And on the 23rd of March, 2012, just a mere 3 months after release, League of Legends hits the number one spot on the list of games in Korea. But why? Why did the Korean player base gravitate so much towards League of Legends? League of Legends is based on a mod of the RTS game Warcraft 3. And even in Korea today, they don't label League of Legends as a MOBA, because they still call it an RTS. This meant that the 10 years of StarCraft skills could in some way translate over to League of Legends. Macro decision-making, micro unit control and ability usage, vision control, and map control. This made picking up League of Legends very, very easy for an entire country that was intimately familiar with the mechanics of StarCraft. The other reason Koreans loved League of Legends was actually one of its biggest differences when compared to StarCraft. StarCraft's main competitive game mode is one versus one, so you have to be at a similar skill level as your friends if you want to have fun playing with them. League of Legends, on the other hand, is five versus five, meaning that groups of friends could all very easily play together. This took advantage of the PC bang culture, as it made the social outing even more social. League of Legends' focus on micro mechanics meant that the high-speed internet of Korea was the perfect place to play. This made the Korean servers synonymous with an extremely high level of micro mechanics, and players always trying to outplay each other no matter what position they're in. As the Korean player base started to become more and more obsessed with League of Legends, the network on Game Net also leaned into the trend and launched the country's first major League of Legends tournament in March of 2012. Korean teams like StarTale and CJ Entus instantly bought into the League of Legends hype, creating their own League of Legends rosters alongside their StarCraft rosters. Riot then doubled down on the popularity in August of 2012 as they created a Korean-specific feature for League of Legends. This feature meant that if you logged into your account to play League of Legends at a PC bang, you would instantly have all of the champions unlocked completely for free. This was a genius move by Riot, leaning into the Korean PC bang culture so the Korean player base felt understood and respected by Riot. All of this culminated into the perfect environment for League of Legends to thrive. And as October rolled around, Season 2 Worlds had finally come. So for a year, Korea had been training and transferring their StarCraft skills, but were still technically a year behind the other regions on game experience. But even still, after just 1 year, the Korean team Azubu Frost made it to the Season 2 Worlds Finals, losing to TPA in the end. But for their first international tournament, Korea had quite a good showing. But over Season 3, the League of Legends scene would really develop and grow as more StarCraft teams would end up making League of Legends rosters. SKT T1, also known as T1 now, and KT Rolster would make their own League of Legends teams and are still competing today, as well as other iconic teams of Samsung Ozone and Jin Air Green Wings. These big teams investing in League of Legends meant that they brought with them 10 years of esports knowledge. The team houses, coaches, mindset, work ethic, and professionalism all carried over into League of Legends. These Korean teams and players took the game very seriously, especially when compared to western teams. Another carryover from StarCraft was the large team rosters. The Korean StarCraft rosters had proven that having lots of players to scrim against each other and sharing their collective knowledge made for exponential improvement. So in the early days of Korean League, many teams would have an A team and a B team, double the talent, consistent scrim partners, and teams could practice strategies amongst themselves without worrying about them being leaked. The B team could also be utilized to trial new players. Faker's first professional game was actually against SKT T1's B team, and his dominant performance is what convinced them to pick him up for the main roster. This A and B team structure meant that the A team, at the end of the year, would be the best players in the organization. This also led to internal competition from players to grind and get better to reach the A team. But Korea didn't just have multiple teams, they also had multiple leagues. OnGameNet was seen as the premier league, but for teams that didn't qualify or got knocked out, there was a secondary league called the NLB. This added even more competition for improvement and made sure that even if teams weren't in the premier league, they were still playing and improving. And in no time at all, Korea had not just caught up to the other regions, but had become the best region to ever play League of Legends. SKT T1 wins 2013 Worlds, then Samsung White wins 2014 Worlds, then SKT T1 wins both 2015 Worlds and 2016 Worlds back-to-back, and then Samsung Galaxy wins 2017 Worlds. So, for Korea, it was five years of straight League of Legends Worlds wins. The gap between Korea and the other regions was huge. All of this dominance was the result of the perfect eSports storm. First, the Asian financial crisis impacts Korea, then the country heavily invests in tech and high-speed internet with a boom of PC bangs across the country. Then StarCraft takes off in Korea and becomes part of Korean culture. The Korean government ends up taking eSports seriously, setting up professional infrastructure. Then League of Legends comes to Korea at the perfect time and Riot Games enters the market very respectfully, and the Korean audience becomes obsessed. So, all that previous StarCraft infrastructure and eSports understanding allows Korea to master the game faster than every other region. But Korea didn't just have some success with every other region catching up, because Korea has continued to improve year after year. The professionalism and infrastructure around eSports continues to grow. Faker has become a household name in Korea. Huge company sponsorships and advertisements with eSports players. Korean eSports teams have their own facilities, PC bangs, and in-person merchandise stores. Riot continues to develop in Korea with the LoL Park venue where the LCK is held, with a Riot merchandise store, PC rooms, and cafe. There are countless League of Legends collaborations in food, drinks, and merchandise in Korea, which reminds me a lot of the StarCraft sponsorships of old, like the iconic Korean Air StarCraft II themed plane. League of Legends has become a large part of Korean culture, as if it were their national game. This has normalized professional gaming so much that the mom of pro player Pays hired a full-time League of Legends tutor when he was in school. Pays was quoted in saying, "At first, he would come over two or three times a week, but eventually, I hit a plateau. Since it was summer break, my tutor stayed with us for a month, watching me play every single day. People in Korea have been seeing gaming success as comparable to when a child is talented at sport or art, getting them the resources they need to succeed. It seems like every other region is playing catch-up to Korea. China is obviously second place when compared to other regions of EU and NA, but Korea and League of Legends are binded together, with Korea winning Worlds four times in the past four years. It seems like the era of Korean dominance is not over yet, and Korea will not stop until they achieve League of Legends perfection.

titre: I Hit GOLD as a Beginner In League of Legends
transcription: I managed to climb from silver four to gold four in just a couple of days, but it was way more difficult than I expected. This wasn't some clean win streak or easy climb like I did in the last video. By the way, if you haven't seen it yet, do check it out. The card is right here. Actually, I lost around 21 games while only winning 20. And even though I was negative, I was still somehow climbing. So, how did this even happen? What went wrong? What went right? And what can you learn from this grind? I'll be showing everything in this video. And make sure you watch till the end because something surprising happens. If you've ever been hard stuck before or felt like your teammates were trash, this might hit a little too close to home. But anyway, let's get into it. If you remember from the last video, I set that the goal for this video was to learn how items and builds work. However, I did not end up learning anything about builds except a little something later in the video. I'll get into that later. Instead, at the very beginning, I tried to learn some new champions. The first one I started out with was Zac. He's this big stinky looking booger thing that heals himself when he eats his goop. To be completely honest, I have no idea why I selected Zac. I think I just read online that he was a good sub for Nunu, but unfortunately, I was not too good with him. But it's okay cuz it's not like I'm going to have to play him in ranked. Or am I? I found out that I'm not very good at hitting skill shots and that my ganks weren't fantastic, but they worked anyway. I was still having fun using [music] his kit though, as you can see in this clip. I was told by several people in the comments on the last video that Nunu was kind of a bad character to start with. This was because he didn't really teach the fundamentals of the jungle, and honestly, I completely agree with that. You can tell that I really have no idea what I'm doing on ganks when I play other champions. The only reason I'm getting away with tomfoolery like this is because this is a swift play. Zac wasn't the only new champion I decided to try out. I also tested around with Xin Zhao, a champion that I really do enjoy playing. However, as I mentioned before, building my experience with Nunu led me to not really know how to play jungle, and I suffered the consequences. But I really just didn't let it get to me because I accept my spot as a beginner in this game. I have a lot to learn if I ever want to hit a top rank. Plays like this are just unacceptable. I think I was going for scuttle crab, but I don't even end up getting it. And even though I genuinely had zero impact on my team, we still ended up winning. And this is going to be a common theme in this video. I unlocked this insane icon, and I play a chill little warm-up match as Nunu and Willump since I'm going to play ranked soon. After playing those horrid games as other champions, I had my mojo back when I played as Nunu and Willump. It feels like I'm playing a completely different game, honestly. My first game back, I go absolutely silly style. I was there on every gank, assisting the kills of every single one of my teammates. And even though I wasn't the one getting the kills, I still felt pretty good about my presence. It's important that you know that in the beginning, I felt like I was important to the team and my contribution made an effort in us winning. I mean, I certainly wasn't carrying or anything, but I think I definitely was helping. I finished my first warm-up game as Nunu and Willump going a whopping 5-0-19. Now it's time to play my first ranked game in silver. I was still riding the high of the win streak I had from the last time I played ranked. I was expecting my rank ups to be just as easy as they were last time. Unfortunately, I was in for a rude awakening. I was doing all the same stuff I was doing in bronze expecting it to work. And I was quickly shown that this is not how you play the game. I didn't understand the importance of clearing my camps. Instead of hitting them up when they refreshed, I instead pushed up into the enemy jungle several times, and while it may work in the early game, I definitely suffered later in the game. I noticed that the enemy jungler would always have higher CS than I had, and I really did not know what I was doing wrong. It wouldn't be until much later that I finally started to grasp the basics [music] of the jungle. Of course, I'll get into that later. This led me to go on my very first losing streak of three back-to-back-to-back games. Lovely. After losing a game on zero LP, I found out that if your MMR is high enough, it will prevent you a couple times from de-ranking. Wait, can you not de-rank in League? You most certainly can, but it takes a while. But let me back up and explain what I just said. You might have heard the term MMR pop up a couple times around my videos. But what is MMR? MMR stands for matchmaking rating. It's a hidden numerical value representing the player's true skill level. In other words, in League of Legends, your visible rank isn't necessarily the full story. Behind it is a hidden MMR, a secret number that actually determines your skill level. If you win games, your MMR goes up. If you lose games, it goes down, obviously. However, if your MMR is higher than your actual rank, you'll gain more LP and climb faster. If it's lower, you'll gain less and drop more easily. This can also lead to double rank ups and other stuff like that. But this isn't relevant to me since it never has happened yet. Hopefully, all that makes sense. Coming up, you're going to see some examples of hidden MMR. Things did eventually start to look good for me after my little loss streak. I definitely locked in after losing three times in a row, and I also definitely got lucky with my teammates, which is important to me because I'm still not very good at the game. There's no chance I'm able to 1v9 because I always play around my teammates. If there was one thing I was good at, it was securing objectives. There were very few times where I didn't secure objectives in the beginning of this climb. And because my lanes were winning and I was securing objectives, I was able to win plenty of games. I won my very first game in silver going 6-4-15. Not too bad. But in my next game, something weird happened. I had to play Zac. I always thought that if I wasn't going to be able to play Nunu, it would be because Nunu got banned, not because my mid laner decided to play Nunu. Thank the actual heavens that my ADC was carrying because there was a zero chance that we were going to win this game. Look at this freaking scoreboard. I was straight goofing around as this booger while Jhin was getting triple kills. If this situation taught me anything, it's that I desperately need to learn how to play a second champion. Fortunately, the other team surrendered, so I didn't have to suffer much longer, and I was rewarded with a solid D+ performance. >> [laughter] >> I did so well that the Jhin even decided to endorse me. Damn, I did genuinely nothing that game. Okay, the momentum was definitely there, and I continued to dominate the next couple of games. My ganks were pretty effective, and I was helping get kills for the team. I think I even pissed off the mid laner because of the amount of times I ganked him. Overall, I was continuing to have a pretty good performance. I had a cheeky little win going 2-3-16. My entire team gave me honor for that performance. Damn, the whole team gave me Yo, after watching this back, I need to get a new laugh. Anyway, I continued the habit of winning, this game most likely being the best game I have played so far. I was kind of going crazy on the assists. Since I was playing tank Nunu, I really couldn't do any damage, so I was relying on my team to finish the job. So, with the momentum that we all had this game, it was pretty easy to end, leading to another victory going 2-2-20. Just like that, I was promoted to silver three, and my confidence was at an all-time high. However, it was not going to be all fun and games. I started to mess up and suffer the consequences. Even when I was only trying to assist my teammates, [music] I ended up making pretty big mistakes and costing us big time. Team fights would be lost, and we would lose objectives. And as you can see here, there was a pretty significant gap between the junglers, a pattern that I would start to notice. It seemed to me that the only times that I would win games was when my team was good enough without me, which in this scenario would be the case. I admit, I got very lucky on this [music] climb. I would continue to get lucky for the next couple of games, winning back-to-back-to-back. And in no time, I was silver two. I started to notice that the lobbies I was getting in were primarily gold and platinum. This was pretty interesting to me, especially considering that I was only silver two. While I definitely was getting better and better the more I played, games started to get much harder. And this is because of the little freaky thing I was talking about earlier, MMR. Since I won so many games in a row, the game just assumed that I was some god to League of Legends, putting me in games that were primarily platinum. So, I was playing against a bunch of platinum players while I'm barely experienced in silver. Not too fun. There are some obvious cons to this, but there are also some pros. I think it's fortunate that I'm getting the experience to play against better players because I am learning quicker because of all the mistakes that I'm making. And the biggest con is probably that this leads me going 0-11-20. My teammates certainly aren't happy about this. And like I said before, this would be completely fine if I got good teammates. Like in this game, I did get good teammates, and we won the game. But this is certainly not a way that you can play League of Legends, especially considering that junglers have high carry potential, and I certainly was not the one doing the carrying. While I still was doing my best and trying to gank, I just was not CSing and keeping up with the enemy jungler. Also, there was a clear skill difference. I was making very poor decisions, and you can see that. Look at the scoreboard. Come on. Ekko totally gapped me. Even in games that I would go on to win, the enemy junglers would still be better than me. Even when I'm only 9 minutes into a game, the enemy jungler has a significant lead. And for the very first time since starting League of Legends, I was introduced into something new. Fear. After getting crushed so easily, I began to fear queuing up. I didn't want to lose more LP, but I really didn't let that stop me and my grind. After all, fear is the mind killer. However, my confidence did not stop me from getting touched by the Hecarim on the other team. There were sometimes where I had my moments, like this clip where I steal a dragon, which I found out I was pretty good at doing. But I never really had an escape route in mind. Dragon or no dragon, I'm a loser. After this game, I actually take a break from playing for an entire week, and that's not by choice. I was actually on spring break. Yes, I'm a college student. I get spring breaks. It was around this time that I was also getting a lot of comments from the previous video, and a lot of you were saying to watch Kesha to learn how to play Nunu. Well, I definitely watched Kesha, and I learned that he plays a little something called AP Nunu. The idea behind AP Nunu is to max out the ability point stat so you can do the most damage and ultimately carry your team. I knew I had to try this out, and honestly, I didn't do too bad my first game playing AP Nunu. Even at the very beginning of the game, I could tell that I had a significant increase in damage output, which gave me more confidence to push and be more aggressive to the enemy team. The only downside that I noticed at first was that I clearly wasn't as fast, and that was because I wasn't using phase rush or anything like that. I mean, honestly, it's a fair trade-off because I can do things like this. You would expect this to be a happy story about how I discovered AP Nunu and [music] conquered ranked. But nope, that's not the case. In fact, I struggled more with ranked than ever. I was still facing the same problems I was facing before. I just simply was not very good at the game, and the other team always made sure to let me know. It was so ingrained to me that I could take much damage as a tank, and when that finally went away, I kind of had to relearn how to play the game. I started to lose a lot of games, like much more than I ever have lost before. Fortunately for me, my hidden MMR was so high that every single game I lost, I only lost 10 LP, and every win, I gained 30 LP. And even though I started to only win games once in a blue moon, it didn't stop me from reaching silver one. I was still very upset with my performance so far. I wanted to be better at the game. So, that night, I watched a bunch of YouTube videos how to play jungle, because I realized I never really watched any of those. So, for the rest of this video, I'm going to try to exemplify what I learned by showing you my worst performance and also my rank up game. I start by doing a full clear in my blue and in my red, pathing towards bottom lane. I figured that this is because the bottom lane is going to be more competitive than the top lane, cuz both champions in the top lane are melee. Since bottom lane's already doing a good job, I don't look for a gank. Instead, I go to the other river to try to find the scuttle crab. Fortunately for me, it is not taken yet. I didn't have any time to base, so I go right back to doing a full clear on blue side and red side. I look for a gank on bot lane, but they're pushed too far back, and I don't really get any value out of it. Since bot lane's occupied, I figure it's safe to take the drake, but I wasn't really making any good time. I go for another gank, my Yasuo dies, and my camps have respawned. I still need to base. I'm just ultimately wasting time. After only full clearing top side, I rush a gank on bottom. I miss the sacra, but I do end up snagging a kill before I peace out. I lose out on the void grubs, but I full clear bottom side. I do yet another gank on bottom lane. This one worked out slightly as well. I attempt a gank on top, but I get juked. Pretty much a couple worthless trades. I lose the hextech drake as I try to gank mid. This interaction does not really go according to my plan, and it results in my first death. I also lose the rift herald objective, but I managed to shut down the Shaco in a little team fight. This is good because it allows me to secure the next drake, no problem. But honestly, it's all downhill from here. I'm not very good at playing mid game. I kept getting so confused by the damn Shaco. This is just going to have to be a match-up that I have to learn. This was probably the worst play I've ever had in all of League of Legends. Since I was dead, nobody could contest the next drake, and you may notice there's a significant gap between me and the Shaco. I was also very hesitant to join team fights, even though that I should be able to do a lot of damage. I sometimes had success, but I was quickly shut down. My ult was super strong, and that's because I was AP Nunu now. And it always seemed to be the Yone that was dying to me. I felt like I did everything I could that game to be able to see us as much as I did and gank as much as I did. Yet, it was to no avail, we got crushed. And honestly, my rank up game was not much better. I once again cleared top first, pathing towards bottom lane. I got the bottom scuttle crab because bot lane did not need a gank. I had a decent gank on Mel after that, but nothing really came of it, she did not die. But at least I got rid of her flash. I hit a quick little base before I go back to top side clearing. I once again cleared top, pathing towards bottom lane, but I skip out on clearing krugs for now. Instead, I ward the dragon, and I look for a gank on bottom lane. Unfortunately, I'm garbage and I did not make anything happen. Twitch dies on my watch, and this is when he starts getting tilted at Brand, the support. I do secure the drake, and I go right to the attack. I realize that our Twitch is our win condition. He's pretty good at the game. Lux and I steal the enemy's blue buff, and I go right back to farming without hitting a base. I low-key get jumped by the enemy jungler while I'm trying to clear the void grubs, but luckily my team had my back, and I was able to get two out of three of them. I hit a base and buy my first item, and you guessed it, back to farming. I also invade the enemy's jungle, which is something that I've been practicing on doing. I also take out the next drake, and do a little bit of a gank on mid lane, where I'm able to take out the thresh, securing my very first kill of this game. The Twitch continues to flame the Brand, and somehow I catch a stray, but I don't let it get to me because I have a W gank on Mel. Look at this. Lux and I are fighting for our lives. Meanwhile, Twitch is still flaming Brand. I literally cannot focus on this game because of the actual argument going on. I mean, low-key Twitch is right, but like, we're playing a game right here. Yo, I'm low-key just trying to clear bottom, bro. Twitch has no chill. I take out the next drake. This time, I actually had an escape plan. After making a terrible play, I died, and I didn't feel like taking it from Twitch. I also low-key started inting. I don't really know why. Like, I die in really embarrassing ways, but it's fine. Even though we're down significantly, we secured the baron. I somehow managed to take down Mordekaiser in his own ult. We then secured the elder dragon, and it's pretty much just wraps from there. Twitch definitely carried this game, and I'm actually pretty grateful for him, even though he's a jerk. God damn. Holy [ __ ] And even though I played terrible in that last game, I still hit gold four. Gold four, baby. Let's go! Thank you all for making it to the end of the video and for your unreal support. I'll continue to make these videos as quick as possible, and I'm sure that I'll hit platinum soon. Anyway, have a great day.

titre: Every Major Event in League of Legends Lore Explained in 27 Minutes
transcription: The creation of Runeterra. A long time ago, Aurelion Sol was forging galaxies and stars around the universe. At one point, he decided to return to one of his earlier creations. He noticed something strange, however. The star that he had created was being orbited by something that he did not forge. It was a world named Runeterra. So, how did Runeterra come into existence? At an unknown point of time, more than 9,000 years before the creation of Noxus, a group of unknown entities used the world runes to create Runeterra. Two separate realms emerged, the physical realm and the spirit realm. The physical realm represents everything material that you can touch and see. It is also the source of all elemental magic. Runeterra also means magic earth, as explained by Aurelion Sol. The spirit realm, on the other hand, is more of a pocket dimension, and as its name suggests, it is the source of all spiritual magic. The first life that appeared in Runeterra were the primordial spirit gods, such as the Nagakabouros. The Nagakabouros is a spirit god entity worshipped in the Bilgewater region. She is also known as the goddess of motion. In current times, Illaoi wields its power. Some of the other entities that emerged at that time were the 10 primordial demons. Each of them represents a primordial emotion that emerged when life was created. The only two of the 10 that are currently known are Fiddlesticks, who represents fear, and Ashlesh, who represents joy. In order to represent all other major events from Runeterra's history, we will use the Noxian calendar to form a timeline. Essentially, everything before the creation of Noxus is represented as before Noxus or BN, and everything after Noxus was created is represented as after Noxus or AN. The Titan War. Around 9,000 years before Noxus, a great war was waged in the lands now known as Ionia. A group of mortals battled a race of sky giants that attacked them from the skies. These sky giants were the Titans, a now extinct species of colossal beings that towered over mountains. So, how did the mortals defeat the Titans? Some of the mortals managed to reach a state of enlightenment, which granted them the powers of the spirit realm. These enlightened mortals became known as the vastaya shurima. The vastaya shurima are an ancient, presumably extinct, species of immensely powerful beings. They were nearly immortal and could use the natural world as a weapon against the Titans. The current vastayans originate from an individual vastaya shurima that had an offspring with humans. The vastayans formed different tribes, and some of them even migrated to the lands outside of Ionia. Certain tribes were also able to retain the magical affinity to the spirit realm. I'm only speculating here, but it's possible that after spreading to the different parts of the world, the vastayans played a big role in introducing magic to the other early inhabitants of Runeterra. War of the three sisters. Around a thousand years later, a feud developed between three sisters in Freljord, Avarosa, Serylda, and Lissandra. The three of them were powerful ancient witches. They had managed to defeat some of the old gods of the north and had united the Freljord. The way they managed to accomplish all of this was because Lissandra had made a deal with the watchers. The watchers are immensely powerful ancient beings from the void. They are the creators of all voidborn, such as Vel'Koz and Bel'Veth. Lissandra conspired with the watchers through her ability to access the spiritual dream realm. In exchange for granting her and her sisters immense power, Lissandra would help them invade the material realm. Even though they now had great power and were the rulers of the Freljord, Avarosa and Serylda started to become skeptical towards their agreement with the watchers. The goal of the watchers was to bring an end to all life, and even though the sisters would be spared thanks to their servitude, they did not want to be the rulers of nothingness. This disagreement between the three sisters grew into an open conflict and subsequent war. Their armies faced at the Howling Abyss, and at the same time, the watchers began to materialize. This caused chaos to ensue as these eldritch horrors began destroying the physical realm. This made Lissandra realize the horrible mistake she had made, and in a last-ditch effort, she drained the yetis of their magic and sealed the watchers in a frost tomb of true ice. In doing so, Lissandra unleashed a cataclysm that froze over most of the northern Freljord. To this day, Lissandra keeps the watchers imprisoned and seeks to find the heart of blue, a powerful magical artifact that will help her create more true ice and keep the watchers in their tomb. The westward migration. The westward migration refers to a period of a thousand years between 6,000 BN to 5,000 BN, when large groups of humans migrated from the eastern part of Runeterra to the west. These humans brought ancient knowledge and wisdom from their lands. Their descendants would become the founders and rulers of the greatest civilizations Runeterra has ever known. This is the modern-day map of Runeterra. But, did you know that this is not the full map? In fact, most of the people that migrated from the eastern part of Runeterra did not come from Ionia, but from the Camavoran continent. It is separated from the rest of the world by the eternal ocean and is the home of the ancient kingdom of Camavor. The continent was initially inhabited by dragons before the first humans settled. Most early records of the kingdom were erased during the Rune Wars. The kingdom itself does not exist in the present timeline. We will cover it later and in more detail when we get to the events of ruination. The rise of the god warriors. This is the period of the golden age of the Shuriman empire. It spanned from 5,000 BN to 2,500 BN and marks the period of time when the Shuriman empire was the biggest growing power in Runeterra. The divine aspects of Mount Targon guided the ancient Shurimans of Nerimazeth in the construction of the first sun disc. They channeled the celestial powers of Aurelion Sol into the disc, which created the first ascended. They were worshipped as living gods and had divine [clears throat] powers that made Shurima into a powerful empire. The warrior queen of the ascended, Setaka, became one of the very first ascended. She wielded the Chalicar, a weapon from the celestial kingdom, which later played a big role in sealing the Darkin. After the destruction of the first sun disc, the Shurimans built an even bigger one in the current Shuriman capital. At that time, the Shurimans also established their first emperor and began absorbing a great deal of nations and cities as vassal states, including Icathia and Oshra Va'an. If this name sounds familiar, it's because this is modern-day Zaun. At around this time, Shurima would begin abolishing all worship of deities other than the ascended, marking them as false idols, such as the Jan'ahrem from Oshra Va'an, also known as Janna. The golden age of the Shuriman empire is also when Nasus and Renekton ascended. To this day, they are hailed as some of Shurima's most powerful heroes. They are also notably some of the ascended that never transitioned into Darkin and remain as ascended to this day. The Void War. One of the other states that Shurima had subjugated was Icathia. The Icathian council submitted countless petitions to the Shuriman emperor hoping for some of its most notable citizens to be chosen for ascension and thus gaining a say in the empire. Hostilities towards Shurima grew after each submission was denied without explanation. At one point, a powerful earthquake in Saibera unearthed a void rift. The Icathian mages took this as an opportunity to weaponize it against the Shuriman oppressors. With their newfound power, the Icathian council announced Icathia's independence from the Shuriman empire. They crowned their own mage king and began killing anyone who was loyal to Shurima. One of the first cities to be liberated was Baizak. During the battle, Icathia's strongest champion, Saijax, even managed to defeat an ascended god warrior. In celebration of their victory, the fallen ascended's head was paraded on the streets of Baizak. This did not get unnoticed by the Shuriman empire, and they responded by sending a group of their most powerful ascended, led by Setaka herself. The ascended were successfully slaying Icathia's armies until, in an attempt to stop them, Icathia's mages accidentally opened a giant void rift, causing countless voidborn to seep into the world. As a Shuriman or Icathian, it didn't really matter. The void was attacking everyone. A massive battle between the voidborn and the ascended ensued, with many ascended, such as Aatrox, experiencing unspeakable horrors. Some of these ascended, including Setaka, would even die in battle. Eventually, the great rift was sealed with the help of the Netherblade. Even though they had managed to stop invasion, this was the beginning of the end of the Shuriman empire. Azir's failed ascension. Azir was the last son of the emperor of Shurima. After his father passed away, Azir was set to take the throne and become emperor. When Azir was young, he met a young slave in Shurima's library. The two quickly became friends, and Azir named him Xerath, even though slaves were not allowed to have names. The two of them grew up together and became as close as brothers. Xerath, however, secretly had other aspirations. He wanted to end all slavery by any means necessary. And even though Azir had promised to help him once he became emperor, Xerath had other plans. After Azir's father and all of his brothers mysteriously passed away, Azir was set to take the throne and become emperor. In the lore, it is implied that Xerath is the reason why the royal family died. Xerath wanted Azir to become emperor so that he could take the power of the Sun Disc and ascend himself instead. So, on the day of Azir's ascension, Xerath used his magic to subvert the ritual. He pushed Azir aside and the Sun Disc's rays fell on Xerath. In an instant, Azir was obliterated while Xerath was remade as a being of pure arcane energy. Shurima's capital was destroyed and many people were killed. Xerath betrayed Azir and by doing so, he plunged Shurima into chaos and despair. Even though it seemed like Azir had died, he had actually managed to ascend but not completely. His soul and body were separated from each other and his spirit laid dormant. Centuries later, he would return and ascend completely. The Great Darkin War. After the events of the Void War, most of the minds of the surviving ascended became broken and twisted. Their souls were scarred and they were left with no purpose. This drove most of them to insanity. Combined with Azir's downfall, Shurima was left with no stable leadership. Most of the ascended had the idea that they were the rightful inheritors of the world and that everyone should worship them. After all, they are ascended gods. So, they started enslaving mortals and conquered any lands that stood before them. The people named their new tyrants the Darkin, meaning the fallen in ancient Shuriman. Many of the Darkin started mastering forbidden arts such as blood magic, which completely altered their appearance. Most of them became completely unrecognizable from the noble warriors they once were. Without a monarchy to establish order or the existential threat of the Void to test them, Aatrox and the other Darkin began to clash with one another, bringing about untold suffering to the mortals they ruled over. In response to this, legend has it that the god warrior Xolanisp aimed to save the lives of the fallen at the request of Tanari. Xolani was initially a healer before she ascended and had a deep mastery of blood magic. It was even rumored she could bring back the dead. Her actions during the war made all other Darkin hate her. However, Xolani too would eventually succumb to madness and she resolved to end the war by using her hemomancy to subjugate all living beings and bring about peace through servitude. The Darkin War lasted for more than a thousand years and stretched to the furthest parts of Runeterra. At one point, even the ancient kingdom of Camavor was threatened. This led the king of Camavor to pledge his loyalty to the Darkin and even offer one of his sons, Vladimir, to the god warriors. There were many attempts to stop the war, even between the Darkin themselves, however, with no success. Eventually, the previous aspect of twilight, Mayisha, gave mortals the knowledge of how to seal the Darkin within their weapons. One of the ascended, Zaun, together with the Kinkou order, managed to seal most of the Darkin and helped put an end to the war. Most Darkin weapons are now scattered around Runeterra. Some of the most powerful Darkin, such as Aatrox, would eventually get revived while others remain sealed until this day. Reign of the Iron Revenant. The reign of the Iron Revenant lasted from 400 before Noxus to 100 before Noxus. It is a historical period where Mordekaiser reigned supreme over Eastern and Central Valoran. 200 years before he became the Iron Revenant though, Mordekaiser was a mortal human. He was no ordinary man though. His name was Sahn-Uzal and he was one of the most ruthless and feared warlords in history. He was a barbarian king who conquered the Great Grass Ocean and slaughtered anyone who was in his way. He believed in only one thing, the strong eat and the weak have nothing to offer their gods. Essentially, he was Runeterra's version of Genghis Khan. He hit and unalived so many people that at one point, he even attracted the demon of ruin, Naafiri, to his encampment. And to top it all off, he defeated the demon and bound him to his will, which made him even more powerful. Despite all of this, he was still a mortal man. Death [clears throat] would also come for Sahn-Uzal. He was not afraid of the afterlife though and met death as an old friend. He believed that he had earned his seat at the gods' table and that only eternal glory was waiting for him. After his passing, he found that there was no eternal glory and there were no gods waiting for him. It was only darkness and emptiness. Because of this, he refused to fade and filled himself with rage. In the empty wasteland, he vowed to forge the kingdom that he was denied. He learned the language of the dead and formed a plan to return to the realm of the living. Over the hundreds of years that he was dead, his ancient conquests faded into legend. After Valoran was ravaged by the Great Darkin War, Sahn-Uzal's whispers from the great beyond reached a coven of powerful mages. They planned to revive Sahn-Uzal and use him as a weapon for their ambitions. Since he lacked a mortal body, they used dark metal plates in the likeness of his old armor to serve as a vessel for his spirit form. In the end, they managed to resurrect him. He was back stronger and more powerful than ever. In fact, he was way stronger than the mages that planned to use him and he simply unalived all of them like it was nothing. He now went by a new name in the language of the dead, Mordekaiser. He quickly amassed a new army and resumed his conquest. He revived Naafiri from the spirit realm and also bound Tybult, a giant fiery demon, to lay waste on his enemies. To his side, he now also had a circle of acolytes, which included LeBlanc. She helped him perform dark rituals for his conquests. After his revival, Mordekaiser forged an empire that lasted for generations. He brought unseen death and destruction everywhere he went. The center of his empire was the Immortal Bastion. It became a symbol of his power and also housed the Darkin forbidden knowledge he had learned in the realm of the dead. At the height of Mordekaiser's reign, LeBlanc and Vladimir formed a secret cabal which served as the foundation of the Black Rose. LeBlanc saw an opportunity to unite the Noxii tribes and to seize the Immortal Bastion. With their secret pact in place, they set a plan in motion to neutralize Mordekaiser. However, unknowingly, they did exactly what Mordekaiser had planned all along. Once his spirit returned to the realm of the dead, he met all of the fading souls that he had killed during his second reign. He forged these souls into the foundation of his afterworld, Mitna Rachnun, where centuries later, he continues to amass power in preparation for his final reign over Runeterra. The Ruination of Helia. 25 years before Noxus was founded, another historical event took place in the southeast part of Runeterra. Shortly after the death of the king of Camavor, his second son, Viego, inherited the throne. Soon after becoming king, Viego fell in love with a seamstress named Isolde. The romance developed quickly and they got married. Viego was so in love with her that he would even start to neglect his kingdom. This resulted in the kingdom's enemies to begin plotting to end Viego's reign. They employed an assassin to take the king's life. Unfortunately for them, Viego's general, Kalista, was at the right place at the right time and managed to save the king's life. In the chaos of the attack though, the assassin's blade that was meant for Viego would pierce the queen instead, dooming her to certain death. In an effort to save her, Viego summoned the greatest scholars and medics of Camavor, but none of them were able to craft a cure for her. Viego read about the waters of life on the Blessed Isles, which were rumored to cure any ailment. He then quickly dispatched Kalista to their presumed location. So, Kalista set sail to the Blessed Isles in search of a cure for the queen. A lot of things happened on her journey, lots of dead ends, but to sum it all up, she first went to the Serpent Isles, but the locals had no information for her. They then met the mysterious seer who told them something cryptic about a golden maiden that would guide them through mist. Afterwards, they randomly met up with Ryze and his mentor, Tyrus of Helia. To Kalista's surprise, the ship that Ryze and Tyrus were on had a figurehead that was a golden maiden. She took this as a sign and asked Tyrus if they could guide them to the Blessed Isles. After some persuading, he agreed. He then showed them the way to the Blessed Isles and Helia. However, this was another dead end. The council of Helia was basically refusing to give Kalista any meaningful answers. A week passed and Kalista wasn't getting anything out of them, so she decided to go back to Camavor. Right before departing from Helia, a mysterious figure approached her. This is where things get interesting. The figure was Erelock Grael, a warden of the thresholds. Grael gave Kalista a small vial containing the waters of life. Kalista was shocked to see that the waters of life were indeed real. Grael said that he could show her the location in exchange for getting a position of influence within the Camavoran kingdom. Kalista was suspicious of Grael's true motives, but hesitantly agreed to the proposal. She set sail back to Camavor to let Viego know about her discovery. However, when she reached the kingdom she found out Viego had descended into madness. The queen had died a few weeks after Kalista had left. After the death of Isolde, Viego had locked himself with her corpse in a tower. Seeing the state the kingdom was in, Kalista refused to share her findings and was therefore thrown in prison because of it. After some persuasion from Hecarim though, she agreed to show them the location of the Blessed Isles. Viego, carrying the corpse of Isolde, set sail to the Blessed Isles along with Kalista and Hecarim's cavalry. Upon reaching the Isles, Viego pleaded with the masters of Helia to help his wife. When the adept scholars of Helia saw the queen's dead body, they declared that nothing could be done for her. Viego was then secretly approached by Arlok Grael. He appealed to the king that the masters had lied to him and that the queen could be saved. They would have to submerge her in the well of ages, which would bring her back from the veil of death. Viego believed him and ordered Kalista to slay anyone who opposed them. Kalista refused and was eventually killed in a battle between her, Hecarim, and her lover, Ledros. Viego and Grael ultimately managed to reach the well of ages deep within Helia. Viego gently lowered the queen's body into the well's waters hoping to restore her to life. What Viego didn't know was that at the bottom of the well, there was a world rune. It grew unstable and caused the queen to be resurrected as a wraith. In pain and anger, she seized Viego's blade and thrust it through his heart. Viego's weapon was no ordinary blade though. It was the magical sword named Sanctity. After both Viego and Isolde sank deep into the well, the sword's magic started to react with the waters of the well, trapping Viego in an endless cycle of destruction and restoration. The waters around his body began to darken and a black mist started to seep from his wounds. The world rune within the waters became wildly unstable. The only thing that could be heard was Grael screaming ruination above the waters. The chamber erupted and an enormous wave of green magical energy engulfed the Isles. From this day forward, they became known as the Shadow Isles. The Rune Wars. The Ruination inadvertently triggered one of, if not the most devastating wars Runeterra has ever seen. Helia was the home of the ancient order whose task was to protect the most dangerous artifacts in Runeterra and to keep the knowledge of the world runes hidden. After the destruction of Helia, these magical artifacts began finding their way into the wrong hands. At around 13 years before Noxus, the leaders of two rival Noxian nations pleaded with Tyrus at the village of Calm. Their encounter escalated and resulted in the complete destruction of Calm and its surrounding territories by the power of two world runes. This cascaded into a series of events where magical warfare erupted. Entire nations were decimated and the world was brought to its knees from the sheer power of the world runes. Ryze's master, Tyrus, tried to use two of the world runes for his own means. After witnessing this, Ryze confronted and unalived him, vowing to never use a world rune and making it his mission to safeguard them from misuse. In Arcane, when Jason Victor Showheimerdinger the Hexcore, he recalls memories of destructive events that seem like the Rune Wars. According to Heimerdinger's Wiki Lore page, he was born in 676 after Noxus, which is way after the Rune Wars happened. So, he's either talking about a different catastrophic event or Arcane is retconning when the Rune Wars happened. In any case, the Rune Wars deteriorated entire civilizations and destroyed most of recorded history from the face of the planet. This is also why the existence of the Camavoran continent was forgotten by the rest of Runeterra and is missing from modern maps. The Rune Wars is also the time when Mihira became the host of the Aspect of Justice and gave birth to Kayle and Morgana. The birth of Noxus and the resurgence of civilization. After a decade of apocalyptic battle, the last of the Noxian tribes were forced to retreat inside the Immortal Bastion for shelter. When they finally emerged, the land beyond had been ravaged, but they survived. These survivors formed modern-day Noxus. Far to the west, the nation founded by the great champion Orlon crowned its first king. Originally settled by refugees from the Rune Wars, it was declared that Demacia would be a sanctuary from magic now and forevermore. Interestingly enough, the early days of Demacian culture were largely shaped by Kayle and Morgana, who became the nation's wing protectors. Although they are not mages, but rather empowered by the celestial powers of the Aspect of Justice, they are still far from normal mortals. The Noxian invasion of Ionia. After a series of forced annexations and the fall of the Draken Gate, the strength of Noxus grew to match its ambitions. The noble houses swore, though it might take a thousand years, to unify all the nations of Runeterra beneath a single banner. The Noxians took action in what they set out to do. After careful planning, Boram Darkwill launched a large-scale occupation of Ionia. One of the biggest battles of the invasion took place at the Placidium of Navori. This is the battle in which Swain lost his arm to Irelia, who was the leader of the resistance. After nearly dying, Swain was approached by the demon Raum. Swain managed to outsmart the demon and took control of him, which saved his life. Afterwards, Swain was dishonorably discharged from the Noxian army and had to return home. With his new-found powers though, Swain enacted a coup in the Noxian capital and disposed of Boram Darkwill. In less than a year, Swain pulled back the majority of Noxian forces from Ionia, leaving only a small occupational force in the Western Isles. Meanwhile, Irelia's revolutionary forces won a striking victory at the battle of Dal Lu Bay. Years later, after the return of Darius, Swain proposed the formation of the Trifarix. This became and still is the governing body of Noxus, where Darius represents might, Swain represents vision, and the mysterious third entity called the Faceless represents guile. The Faceless, as you may have guessed, is LeBlanc. This, of course, is a very summed-down version of the Noxian invasion of Ionia and Swain's rise in the Noxian leadership. There are so many other notable events that happened in Ionia because of the invasion, like the formation of the Order of Shadows, Yasuo unaliving his brother Yone, and so on. If you'd like me to make a more detailed video about the Noxian invasion or the Noxian political situation, please let me know in the comments. I hope you enjoyed this breakdown of the most major events in League's lore, and I'll see you in the next one. Take care.
